rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being unsure whether the equipment is truly running cleanly after restart
rows.imp_rat: Being unsure whether the equipment truly runs cleanly after restart creates emotional unease tied to the aseptic stakes of this segment.
rows.sat_rat: Post-restart monitoring gives partial confidence, but early-run cleanliness confirmation is not always immediate.

rows.source_job: feel sure the repair will hold
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that the restored equipment will hold up once back in production
rows.imp_rat: Personal doubt that the repair will hold weighs on the technician emotionally, though the job is judged on outcomes more than feelings.
rows.sat_rat: Verification evidence provides some reassurance, but lingering uncertainty about durability is common after complex fixes.

rows.source_job: feel sure the repair will hold
rows.stmt: Reduce the likelihood of lacking evidence that the repair will withstand normal production load
rows.imp_rat: Having evidence the repair will withstand production load helps the technician feel confident and defend their work, a supporting emotional need.
rows.sat_rat: Functional tests supply some evidence, but load-durability proof is often anecdotal rather than demonstrated.

rows.stmt: Reduce the likelihood of being seen as slow to bring critical equipment back to life when production is waiting
rows.imp_rat: Being seen as slow when production waits stings the technician's go-to identity, tying status to restoration speed.
rows.sat_rat: Techs often meet expectations, but parts and approval delays outside their control can still make them look slow.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of high-visibility repair failures that erode leadership's trust in me as the go-to technician
rows.imp_rat: High-visibility repair failures that erode leadership trust matter to the technician's standing, though they are less frequent than routine issues.
rows.sat_rat: Trust is usually preserved, but the occasional public failure with no supporting evidence can damage it under today's workflow.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of idle craft labor time left unassigned during an available maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Leaving craft hours idle in a window wastes a scarce, expensive resource, but for the planner it ranks below hitting downtime windows since labor cost is dwarfed by lost sterile production.
rows.sat_rat: Typical CMMS scheduling boards give partial capacity visibility but rarely balance loads dynamically, so pockets of idle time regularly slip through.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in maintenance intervals applied across functionally similar equipment in the population
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent maintenance intervals across similar equipment produces uneven reliability and inefficiency, a moderate concern for strategy definition.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized PM templates align many intervals, though legacy exceptions and site variation leave residual inconsistency.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that the recommended methods will reduce unplanned failure risk to an acceptable level
rows.imp_rat: Confidence that the recommended methods will actually cut failure risk to acceptable levels is the emotional payoff that lets this engineer stand behind a recommendation.
rows.sat_rat: Historical performance data provides some reassurance but residual forecast and risk uncertainty leaves lingering doubt in most recommendations.

rows.source_job: feel reassured the choice will hold up
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the endorsed resources failing to keep mechanical integrity intact once equipment returns to service
rows.imp_rat: The reassurance that endorsed resources will hold up once equipment returns to service matters because this engineer's name is attached to that durability.
rows.sat_rat: Post-maintenance verification and qualification give partial reassurance, but early-life failures after return-to-service still undermine full confidence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a recommendation being followed by an unplanned failure that undermines authority with operations and leadership
rows.imp_rat: A recommendation followed by an unplanned failure directly undermines this engineer's authority with operations and leadership, the currency of their influencer role.
rows.sat_rat: Documented risk-based justification offers some protection, but when failures follow a recommendation the status quo does little to preserve standing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which a missed failure mode erodes standing as the authoritative reliability expert
rows.imp_rat: A missed failure mode publicly erodes this engineer's standing as the authoritative reliability voice, which is core to the status job driving their influence.
rows.sat_rat: Peer review and RCA processes cushion reputational blows somewhat, but a single missed mode still visibly dents authority, so satisfaction is only moderate.

rows.source_job: feel certain the call was sound
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of second-guessing a requalification scope decision after the intervention is closed
rows.imp_rat: Second-guessing a closed scope decision drains confidence but is a lower-stakes emotional concern relative to active compliance risks.
rows.sat_rat: Documented risk rationale offers some closure, but lingering doubt persists because scope calls lack objective confirmation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time spent negotiating acceptable terms when preferred commercial terms cannot be met
rows.imp_rat: Time lost negotiating fallback terms delays the PO and ties up the manager, though it is a routine sourcing friction rather than a compliance risk.
rows.sat_rat: Standard contract templates and negotiation playbooks give partial support, but bespoke regulated-service terms still require slow case-by-case bargaining.

rows.stk: Plant Engineering/Maintenance Budget Owner (Site Capital & OPEX)
rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent verifying that a maintenance spending request is valid before authorizing the release
rows.imp_rat: Time spent verifying request validity is administrative drag on my authorization job, meaningful but not existential.
rows.sat_rat: Standard approval templates speed some verification, yet confirming technical justification still often requires back-and-forth with engineering.

rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of repeat failures on equipment I restored that would undermine my standing as the dependable restorer
rows.imp_rat: Repeat failures on equipment they restored directly threaten the technician's reputation as the dependable restorer.
rows.sat_rat: Experienced techs generally maintain solid track records, so their standing is reasonably intact under current practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of dispatching a technician to the wrong equipment location or work order
rows.imp_rat: A tech sent to the wrong asset or work order wastes a downtime window, but clear work-order location data usually keeps this from being frequent.
rows.sat_rat: Modern CMMS work orders carry equipment IDs and locations, so this is handled reasonably well though gowning zones can still cause misroutes.

rows.source_job: feel confident the right tech is on it
rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of second-guessing a dispatch decision after the technician is already on the job
rows.imp_rat: Second-guessing a dispatch after the fact is a real but secondary emotional concern relative to actually getting the qualified tech there.
rows.sat_rat: Once qualification and location are confirmed, planners feel moderately settled, though limited status feedback keeps some doubt alive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty in attributing maintenance cost to the equipment reliability it delivers
rows.imp_rat: Cost-attribution accuracy is largely a finance/maintenance concern and only indirectly touches this manager's output focus.
rows.sat_rat: Attribution of cost to delivered reliability is weak today, but this manager rarely relies on it directly.
